name: Julia Series
description: The Julia Series is Fracktal's next-generation desktop 3D printer, redesigned from the ground up for printing performance materials — carbon fibre composites, ABS composites, and similar engineering-grade feedstocks. Primary target market: industrial tooling, jigs, and fixtures.
objectives: Produce a desktop 3D printer capable of reliably printing carbon fibre and ABS composites
Implement an enclosed heated chamber to handle high-performance materials
Deploy a CAN-bus toolhead architecture for plug-and-play replaceability
Integrate an eddy-current inductive sensor for automatic calibration and bed levelling
Deliver a parametric, scalable chassis that supports Single and IDEX variants
scope_in: Julia Single — CoreXY, 256×256×300 mm build volume
Julia IDEX — Hybrid CoreXY with dual carriages
Eddy-current inductive sensor for the toolhead
PCB update for MKS Robin v3 compatibility
scope_out: Build volume larger than 256×256×300 mm in v1

milestones.milestone: CAD Sprint 1 Complete (chassis, back panel, HEPA, heater)

dependencies: MKS Robin v3 PCB must be completed before firmware bring-up
Eddy-current sensor selection (custom or off-shelf) gates carriage CAD
IDEX variant depends on single-extruder baseline validation

functional_requirements.req: Print carbon fibre composite filament (CF-PLA, CF-PETG, CF-Nylon)
Enclosed and heated chamber reaching at least 50°C ambient
CAN-bus toolhead interface — plug-and-play replacement in < 5 min
Eddy-current sensor calibrates pressure advance automatically per material
CoreXY motion system with IDEX extension for dual-material printing
HEPA filter for composite particulate capture
Parametric chassis — volume change requires only model rescale

nonfunctional_requirements.req: Print quality at 0.2 mm layer height with CF composite

constraints: Toolhead CAN bus: must be compatible with off-shelf EBB-series or equivalent
Chassis must be manufacturable with Fracktal's in-house sheet metal and FDM capabilities
Cost target for BOM (single unit) to be defined

personas.name: Industrial tooling engineer
personas.description: Needs structural jigs, fixtures, and brackets printed from CF composites at desktop scale, without sending to a bureau.

personas.name: R&D engineer
personas.description: Iterates on composite prototype parts, needs consistent mechanical properties across prints.

architecture: The Julia Series uses a fully enclosed CoreXY gantry. The chassis is a sheet-metal and aluminium extrusion frame, designed parametrically so all variants share the same component tree. Electronics live on a central control board (MKS Robin v3 or equivalent), with toolhead electronics distributed over CAN bus to the toolhead PCB. Klipper runs on a Raspberry Pi mounted inside the enclosure.
how_it_works:
Motion: CoreXY H-bot with dual stepper motors driving the X/Y gantry. Z is driven by a single leadscrew (single) or dual leadscrews (IDEX).

Toolhead: A CAN-bus module on the toolhead carries the hotend thermistor, heater, part-cooling fan, and the eddy-current inductive sensor. The sensor measures nozzle displacement relative to the extruder body to calibrate pressure advance and perform bed levelling without a separate probe.

Chamber: A sheet-metal enclosure with optional active heating (resistive heater + thermistor) and optional HEPA filter for composite particulate. Desiccant management is separate (see Penrose Dryer project).

Firmware: Klipper, configured via printer.cfg. CAN bus toolhead uses canboot bootloader.

design_decisions.title: CAN-bus toolhead over cable loom
design_decisions.context: Previous printers used a drag-chain cable loom — fragile, hard to replace, limits toolhead modularity.
design_decisions.decision: All toolhead electronics communicate over CAN bus (2-wire + power). Single toolhead PCB.
design_decisions.rationale: Reduces wiring complexity, enables plug-and-play toolhead swap, aligns with Klipper CAN support (katapult/canboot).
design_decisions.consequences: Requires custom or semi-custom CAN toolhead PCB. EBB36/42 off-shelf options evaluated as baseline.

design_decisions.title: Eddy-current inductive sensor as bed probe + pressure advance calibrator
design_decisions.context: CR Touch / BLTouch probes are slow and wear-prone. Linear advance calibration is currently manual.
design_decisions.decision: Toolhead-mounted eddy-current sensor (nozzle-relative displacement) for both bed levelling and pressure advance.
design_decisions.rationale: Eliminates separate probe, enables automatic material profiling, increases repeatability.
design_decisions.consequences: Sensor PCB design or off-shelf selection needed. Firmware integration with Klipper required.

design_decisions.title: Parametric chassis design
design_decisions.context: Fracktal has multiple printer variants with different build volumes. Separate full CAD models for each are expensive to maintain.
design_decisions.decision: Single parametric chassis — build volume is a top-level parameter. All variants derive from the same model.
design_decisions.rationale: Reduces CAD maintenance burden; new variants only require parameter changes, not redesign.
design_decisions.consequences: Initial CAD effort higher; requires disciplined parametric modelling practice.

subsystems.name: Toolhead
subsystems.description: The toolhead carries the hotend, extruder motor, part-cooling fan, and eddy-current sensor. All electronics interface via CAN bus to the main board.
specs: Eddy-current inductive sensor for pressure advance calibration and bed levelling
Compatible with E3D Revo or equivalent hotend ecosystem
Support for nozzle sizes 0.25 mm to 0.8 mm
known_issues: CAN toolhead PCB: custom vs off-shelf (EBB36/42) decision pending (owner: Vijay)

subsystems.name: Motion System
subsystems.description: CoreXY H-bot gantry for XY. Z driven by single leadscrew (Julia Single) or dual leadscrews (Julia IDEX). Hybrid CoreXY IDEX variant adds a second carriage on an independent Y rail.
specs: CoreXY — XY speeds up to 300 mm/s target
IDEX: Klipper IDEX mode, independent dual carriages on shared gantry
known_issues: IDEX Y-axis motion: belt vs leadscrew not yet decided (owner: Vijay)
Z-axis re-arrangement may be required depending on motor cooling separation (Sougata)

subsystems.name: Chamber Heating & Filtration
subsystems.description: Enclosed chamber with optional active heating for ABS/CF printing and optional HEPA filtration for composite particulate capture.
specs: Target chamber temperature: 50°C minimum (60°C+ TBD for CF composites)
HEPA filter: smaller form factor with active air circulation (redesign in progress)
Compact resistive heater addition (CAD in Sprint 1)
known_issues: Chamber heater target temperature not finalised: 60°C for ABS or higher for CF composites? (owner: Vijay)

subsystems.name: Electronics & PCB
subsystems.description: Central control board is an updated legacy PCB targeting MKS Robin v3 compatibility. CAN bus backbone connects to toolhead PCB.
specs: CAN bus interface (TWAI or external CAN transceiver)
Raspberry Pi 4 as Klipper host (mounted inside enclosure)
Power supply: 24V main, 5V for Pi and fans
known_issues: PCB update for MKS Robin v3 is URGENT — due 2026-06-07 (owner: Vijay)

subsystems.name: Firmware
subsystems.description: Klipper firmware on Raspberry Pi. Toolhead firmware on CAN PCB using katapult (canboot) bootloader. printer.cfg to be built alongside hardware bring-up.
specs: Katapult bootloader on CAN toolhead PCB
Pressure advance auto-calibration via eddy-current sensor integration
known_issues: Firmware config cannot start until PCB and toolhead PCB are finalised

open_questions.question: Toolhead CAN PCB: custom in-house design or off-shelf EBB36/42?
IDEX second carriage Y-axis motion: belt or leadscrew?
Chamber heater target temperature: 60°C (ABS) or higher for CF composites?
Eddy-current sensor: select off-shelf unit or design in-house PCB?

risks.risk: CAN toolhead PCB decision delays toolhead CAD and firmware start
risks.mitigation: Evaluate EBB36 as drop-in baseline; custom PCB as stretch goal

risks.risk: PCB update for MKS Robin v3 takes longer than 1 sprint
risks.mitigation: Time-box to 2 days; escalate if blocked

risks.risk: Eddy sensor firmware integration is non-trivial in Klipper
risks.mitigation: Prototype firmware integration early on existing machine

risks.risk: IDEX Y-axis design adds significant CAD scope
risks.mitigation: Complete and validate Single variant first; IDEX is phase 2
